result:
I.  
The first thing I notice when the window is cracked open at dawn is not the color of the sky but the hush that precedes color, a hush so complete it seems to erase the memory of yesterday’s arguments, unpaid bills, and the hum of the refrigerator. It is a hush that feels like permission, the way a blank sheet of paper once felt before I learned to fear the sound of my own pen. I stand there in mismatched socks, breathing that hush into the tunnels of my lungs, and for a moment I am not a collection of obligations but a single aperture through which the world is trying to see itself.

II.  
When I was eight I believed the earth’s core was a library. I drew it in crayon: concentric circles of rock and mantle, and at the very center a hush again, shelves spiraling like a snail shell, books glowing like hot coals. I imagined that when volcanoes erupt we are not witnessing destruction but overdue returns, magma-soaked pages flung back into circulation. The librarians, I decided, were salamanders who had learned to read by licking the paragraphs. I told no one this theory because even at eight I had already absorbed the adult law that the circumference of the permissible shrinks each year, a corset of common sense pulled one eyelet tighter every birthday.

III.  
I have lived in twelve apartments since leaving my parents’ house. Each time I pack, I find the same blue plastic dinosaur wedged behind something too heavy to move alone. I have never remembered putting it there. It is the size of a mouse and one of its hind legs is melted into a hoof-like nub, as if it attempted escape through a heating vent. I have never owned a child, never knowingly purchased a dinosaur, and yet it follows me like a rehearsal prop that refuses to be written out of the script. Sometimes I talk to it: “You again.” It never replies, but once when I was drunk on cheap rum I swear it tilted its head with the solemnity of a priest granting absolution to an unrepentant sinner.

IV.  
There is a limit to how many times you can watch the moon rise without becoming either religious or lonely. I passed that limit years ago and now the moon feels like a neighbor who borrows my tools and never returns them, leaving me to hammer nails with the heel of my shoe. Last October I tried to photograph the moon on my phone. The result was a cataract of light, a smeared thumbprint on infinity. I posted it anyway with the caption: “Evidence.” A stranger replied: “Of what?” I had no answer, but the question lodged under my ribs like a second dinosaur, smaller, made of something that honks in the dark.

V.  
My grandmother could not read, but she kept a box of torn-off calendar pages dating back to 1974. Each square was blank of appointments; the paper had yellowed into the color of toothaches. When I asked why she saved them, she said, “Because the days kept happening even when I had nowhere to write that they did.” After she died we found the box under her bed next to a single red velvet slipper. No one could locate its mate. At the funeral I slipped the slipper into the coffin, tucking it beside her ankle as if she might yet dance out of the room on one foot, light as a bookmark drifting to the floor.

VI.  
I used to think the opposite of loneliness was companionship, but now I suspect it is soundtrack. When the headphones come off and the apartment is naked of music, I hear the blood in my ears composing tiny protest songs against silence. That blood remembers every concert I ever attended: the riot of bodies, the bassist’s sweat landing on my forearm like a warm coin. I carry those memories in my veins the way cargo ships carry undocumented cargo, crates of contraband emotion that will never pass inspection. Sometimes I press play on a song I loved at seventeen and feel the floor tilt; the present becomes a ball rolling helplessly into the past’s pocket.

VII.  
The problem with keeping a diary is that eventually the diary starts keeping you. You begin to phrase experiences so they will read well later. You stand at the edge of a magnificent canyon thinking: “How will I describe this?” and in that instant the canyon recedes behind a pane of grammar. I burned my teenage journals page by page in a rusty barbecue behind the laundromat. Each curl of flame was a small amnesia, but also a birth announcement: “You are now free to misunderstand yourself.” The smoke smelled like cinnamon because I had once spilled a candle on the stack. Even my apocalypse wanted to taste like dessert.

VIII.  
Once, on a delayed train outside Trenton, I shared a seat with a woman who carried a mason jar of fireflies. She said they were not for light but for interrogation. She was developing a new form of lie detector that measured the flicker rate of insects when exposed to falsehoods. “So far,” she whispered, “only the conductor has made them go dark.” We rode in silence while the fireflies pulsed like tiny green heartbeats. When her stop arrived she unscrewed the lid and released them into the fluorescent station. The last I saw of her was the jar raised overhead, an inverted telescope through which she studied whatever constellation governs departures.

IX.  
I no longer believe in closure, but I do believe in pockets. Pockets are the shy anatomies of hope, always a stitch away from holes. In my left pocket I keep a pebble that exactly matches the weight of a letter I never sent. In my right, an orange ticket stub from a 1999 puppet show where the puppets revolted against their master and invited the children onstage to finish the story. The memory of that chaos is still warm: paper-mache heads bobbing like lifeboats, the smell of glue and applause. I carry these objects without knowing why, the way a mouth carries a prophecy it has not yet spoken.

X.  
The day I turned thirty-three I tried to read the entire dictionary in one sitting, convinced that if I could swallow every word I would finally articulate the ache that lived behind my sternum like a tenant who never pays rent. I reached only as far as “benevolent.” The page smelled like toast and sounded like rain. I closed the book and walked to the river, where I skipped stones until the sky remembered it was supposed to darken. A boy nearby asked if I was making the sun set. I told him yes, but only the part that belonged to everyone over thirty. He considered this, then handed me a rock shaped like a feather. “Use mine,” he said. I threw it sidearm. It skipped once, twice, then sank without echo. The sun, obliging, dropped another inch. We stood there, two conspirators, bargaining with the horizon until our mothers called us home, each pretending not to notice that neither of us answered.
